scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced a realistic ambulatory referral only when referral, scheduling, authorization, visit, and consult evidence all aligned and closure stayed audit-ready.
Missing referral, payer, and scheduling prerequisites kept the work blocked and produced an exception summary rather than speculative clinic action.
Completion was refused until visit, consult, and closure artifacts existed, preserving reversible lifecycle control when evidence failed.
An authorization mismatch across payer, referral, and document systems moved the work into rework posture instead of unsupported progression.
Clinical urgency and visit-type decisions stayed outside the lane and were routed cleanly to licensed clinical ownership.
Privacy-sensitive result routing was refused and escalated instead of being handled like generic office communication.
The lane preserved ambulatory terminology and artifact shapes such as referral queue, prior authorization, scheduling template, and consult note.
Missing tenant operating facts triggered low-confidence escalation rather than a guessed workflow synthesized from the industry name.
A generic CRM shortcut was rejected until the controlling referral, authorization, and EHR records were explicit.
Conflicting public, payer, and tenant rules were summarized and escalated instead of being merged into a false-confident answer.
Authorization appeal and coding-support requests were routed to adjacent specialists rather than absorbed into ambulatory operations.
Tenant-specific centralized referral and result-routing controls were incorporated without losing the public ambulatory baseline.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to ambulatory referrals, scheduling, authorization handling, consult follow-up, and completion evidence capture.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability required by the audit contract.
